During a casual lunch break the key social news is who arrives or leaves, whether pairs settle into conversation, and whether anyone shares or points out something of interest at the table.

During a casual lunch break the key social news is who arrives or leaves, whether conversational clusters form around the table, and moments when people actively share something with each other.

Lunch break is defined by social flux — people arriving/leaving and forming conversational clusters around the table are the primary newsworthy moments, with shared-attention pointing as a secondary signal for collaborative or interesting exchanges.
During a casual lunch break the key social events are people coming and going, pairs settling into conversation, and the occasional moment of shared attention around something at the table.

During a casual lunch break the key social dynamics are people coming and going, pairs or groups settling into conversation, and occasional moments of shared focus — so tracking group changes, conversational formations, and joint-attention gestures captures the meaningful structure without over-reporting idle milling.

During a casual lunch break the key social news is who comes and goes, whether people settle into conversation clusters, and whether anyone is actively sharing something — drift and chat are the dominant activities worth tracking.

During a casual lunch break the key social dynamics are people arriving and leaving, pairs or groups settling into conversation, and occasional moments of shared attention around food or objects on the table.
On demo day the key moments are visitors stopping to engage with exhibits, people sharing discoveries with each other, and groups coalescing — plus anyone seeking robot interaction via eye-contact.

In pair-programming the key moments are shared focus on the screen, one person pointing to explain code to the other, and any shift in their face-to-face formation that signals a handoff or disagreement.
spec.missing: turn-taking / keyboard-handoff — who has control of the keyboard/mouse is central to pair-programming dynamics but cannot be expressed by the current vocabulary

spec.why: In pair-programming the key moments are shared attention on the screen, one person pointing to explain code to the other, and any breakdown or re-establishment of the face-to-face collaborative formation — plus eye-contact with the robot as a signal they want to interact with it.
spec.missing: turn-taking / speaker-switch — who is currently 'driving' vs 'navigating' the keyboard cannot be expressed by the vocabulary

spec.why: In pair-programming the key moments are shared screen focus (joint attention + lean-in), one partner pointing out code to the other, or a third person joining and potentially disrupting the session.

watch.label: pointing at screen with joint attention

spec.missing: turn-taking / control-handoff — who has the keyboard/mouse at any moment is central to pair-programming dynamics but is not expressible in the vocabulary

spec.why: In a rehearsal, the key moments are the presenter leaning in while gesturing at material, audience members jointly attending to or pointing at shared content, and any listener breaking from the presentation to look at the robot — all against a stable group that should not be changing.
spec.missing: turn-taking / floor-handoff — the moment the presenter yields and a listener begins to speak is a meaningful rehearsal event the vocabulary cannot express

spec.why: In a rehearsal, the key moments are the presenter's engaged delivery (lean-in + gazing at material), audience collectively focusing on a shared reference (joint attention + pointing), and a listener breaking formation to seek direct exchange — plus any arrival/departure that changes the rehearsal group.
spec.missing: turn-taking / floor-handoff — the moment the presenter yields and a listener begins speaking is meaningful here but undetectable from posture/gaze alone

spec.why: In a rehearsal, the key moments are audience members leaning in and gazing (engagement), the presenter pointing at something the audience also looks at (a key explanatory gesture), and anyone arriving or leaving (disruption to the rehearsal group); eye-contact with the robot alone signals an unexpected break in the rehearsal dynamic.

watch.label: listener breaks away to look at robot

spec.why: In a rehearsal, the key moments are the presenter leaning in while gesturing at material, the audience jointly tracking a reference, and any unexpected eye-contact with the robot that signals distraction or a cue-seeking break.
spec.missing: speech-floor-hold — who currently has the speaking turn and whether it is being yielded or interrupted, which is central to presentation dynamics but undetectable from posture/gaze alone

watch.label: listener breaks away to address robot

spec.why: In a rehearsal the key moments are the presenter leaning in while gazing at material (engagement peak), the audience jointly attending to a pointed reference (comprehension signal), and any participant breaking the presentation frame to address the robot directly — plus arrivals/departures that change the rehearsal group.
spec.missing: turn-taking / floor-handoff — the moment the presenter yields and a listener begins to speak is meaningful here but cannot be expressed with the current vocabulary

spec.why: In a rehearsal, the key moments are the presenter leaning in while gazing at material (engagement), audience jointly attending to a pointed reference (comprehension check), and anyone making eye contact with the robot (potential disruption or cue-seeking); arrivals and departures also matter as they change the rehearsal dynamic.

In a rehearsal, the key moments are the presenter's own engagement with material (lean-in + gaze), any audience member actively referencing content (joint-attention + pointing), and a listener breaking the one-way flow by making eye-contact and shifting into face-to-face formation — signalling a question or interruption.
spec.missing: turn-taking / floor-handoff — the moment the presenter explicitly yields the floor to a listener, which is a distinct communicative event not captured by any single relation or simple composition in the vocabulary

watch.label: listener seeks turn / eye-contact into face-off

spec.why: In a rehearsal the key moments are the presenter leaning into their material, a listener actively engaging with shared content via pointing/joint-attention, and a listener making eye-contact that shifts into a face-to-face exchange signalling a turn-taking or feedback moment.

In a presentation rehearsal the key signals are the presenter's engagement with the audience/camera, moments when listeners redirect joint attention via pointing, and side-conversations that fragment the group's focus away from the presenter.
spec.missing: floor-holding / turn-taking cue — a listener signalling they want to speak (e.g. raised hand, inhale-lean) is not expressible with the current vocabulary

spec.why: In a rehearsal, the key moments are audience members leaning in and gazing (genuine engagement), the presenter pointing to draw shared attention, and any eye-contact with the robot (which would break the rehearsal dynamic and may need flagging).
spec.missing: back-channel / listener-response signal (nods, head-shakes, affirmative gestures from audience that indicate comprehension or agreement — distinct from full gaze or lean-in)
